Human beings are often infected by microorganisms such as bacteria, molds, yeasts and viruses present in their living envi- ronment.

Human beings are often infected by microorganisms such as bacteria, molds, yeasts and viruses present in their living envi- ronment. Because of the emergence and increase in the number of multi drug-resistant microorganisms and also the continuing emphasis on health-care, scientists are trying to develop new effec- tive antimicrobial agents that overcome the resistance of those microorganisms.

Because of the emergence and increase in the number of multi drug-resistant microorganisms and also the continuing emphasis on health-care, scientists are trying to develop new effec- tive antimicrobial agents that overcome the resistance of those microorganisms. The emergence of nanoscience and nanotechnol- ogy in the last decade represents opportunities for exploring the bactericidal effect of metal nanoparticles.

The bactericidal effect of metal particles has been attributed due to their small size and high surface to volume ratio (Morones et al., 2005). Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are attractive, because they are non-toxic to the human body at low concentrations and have broad spectrum antibacterial actions (Baker, Pradhan, Pakstis, Pochan, & Shah, 2005).

Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are attractive, because they are non-toxic to the human body at low concentrations and have broad spectrum antibacterial actions (Baker, Pradhan, Pakstis, Pochan, & Shah, 2005). The present study describes the mechanism of formation of sil- ver nanoparticles using carboxy methyl cellulose (CMC), which has been widely used as a good stabilizer in the synthesis of metal nanoparticles in an aqueous medium (Liu, He, Durham, Zhao, & Roberts, 2008).

The present work was conducted to develop cost effective fabrication of spherical silver nanoparticles at ambient temperature and in short reaction time. Also we have investigated the antimicrobial activity of the synthesized silver nanoparticles against both gram positive and gram negative bacteria.

Also we have investigated the antimicrobial activity of the synthesized silver nanoparticles against both gram positive and gram negative bacteria. The result signifies that silver nanoparticles fabricated using CMC are suitable for formulation of new types of bactericidal materials.

Materials Silver nitrate (AgNO3), Trisodium citrate (C6H5O7Na3), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Sodium carboxy methyl cellulose (NaCMC), Nutrient agar, Nutrient broth, Mueller Hinton agar, Agar agar, and standard antibiotic discs were purchased from Himedia Private Limited, Mumbai. Milli-Q water was used for the fabrication of nanoparticles and also for preparation of all other aqueous solu- tions throughout the experiment.
Milli-Q water was used for the fabrication of nanoparticles and also for preparation of all other aqueous solu- tions throughout the experiment. 2.2.

Synthesis of silver nanoparticles using CMC (Vasileva, Donkova, Karadjova, & Dushkin, 2011) All glassware’s used in the experiment were cleaned well using nitric acid and rinsed with double distilled water, followed by ace- tone for prior to use. The aqueous dispersion of silver nanoparticles was synthesized by the chemical reduction of AgNO using 3 D- glucose as an environmentally benign reducing agent, CMC as a non-toxic stabilizing agent and NaOH as a base reaction catalyst.

The aqueous dispersion of silver nanoparticles was synthesized by the chemical reduction of AgNO using 3 D- glucose as an environmentally benign reducing agent, CMC as a non-toxic stabilizing agent and NaOH as a base reaction catalyst. Freshly prepared aqueous solution of CMC (0.2% [w/v], 32 ml) was stirred for 15 min.

2.3. Characterization of CMC stabilized AgNPs The CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles were roughly moni- tored by visual inspection of the reaction solution by color change (Hebeish, El-Rafie, Abdel-Mohdy, Abdel-Halim, & Emam, 2010).

Characterization of CMC stabilized AgNPs The CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles were roughly moni- tored by visual inspection of the reaction solution by color change (Hebeish, El-Rafie, Abdel-Mohdy, Abdel-Halim, & Emam, 2010). UV–vis spectra of the nanoparticles suspension was recorded a on Shimadzu dual beam spectrophotometer (UV-1650 PC) operated at a resolution of 1 nm at the wavelengths of 200–800 nm.

UV–vis spectra of the nanoparticles suspension was recorded a on Shimadzu dual beam spectrophotometer (UV-1650 PC) operated at a resolution of 1 nm at the wavelengths of 200–800 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of nanoparticles was carried on Shimadzu FT-IR spectrophotometer at the range between 4000- 550 cm-1(Pandey, Goswami, & Nanda, 2012).

The diffraction intensities were compared with the standard JCPDS files (Valodkar, Bhadoria, Pohnehkar, Mohan, & Thakore, 2010). Mor- phology of the synthesized silver nanoparticles was observed with the scanning electron microscope (JSM 35 CF JEOL) operated at a resolution of 60 Å at 15 kv (Hebeish, Hashem, Abd El-Hady, & Sharaf, 2013).

Mor- phology of the synthesized silver nanoparticles was observed with the scanning electron microscope (JSM 35 CF JEOL) operated at a resolution of 60 Å at 15 kv (Hebeish, Hashem, Abd El-Hady, & Sharaf, 2013). The quantitative distribution of polysaccharide stabilized silver nanoparticles was investigated by ED’s analysis (JSM 35 CF JEOL) in a resolution of 60 Å with the magnification of 5 k (Hebeish et al., 2013).

The quantitative distribution of polysaccharide stabilized silver nanoparticles was investigated by ED’s analysis (JSM 35 CF JEOL) in a resolution of 60 Å with the magnification of 5 k (Hebeish et al., 2013). AFM of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles was mea- sured and characterized using Atomic Force Microscopy (Nanonics imaging MN1000), which measured the atomic range of particles using tapping mode (Sharma, Yngard, and Lin, 2009).

AFM of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles was mea- sured and characterized using Atomic Force Microscopy (Nanonics imaging MN1000), which measured the atomic range of particles using tapping mode (Sharma, Yngard, and Lin, 2009). 2.4.
2.4. Antibacterial studies of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles 2.4.1.

Antibacterial studies of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles 2.4.1. Bacterial culture Both gram positive and gram negative human bacterial pathogens such as Bacillus cereus (MTCC 619), Escherichia coli (MTCC 4296), Klebsiella pneumoniae (MTCC 4030), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 424), Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC 3160), Staphy- lococcus epidermidis (MTCC 435), Vibrio vulnificus (MTCC 1146) and Salmonella typhimurium (MTCC 3216) were procured from the Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC), Chandigarh, India.

us cereus (MTCC 619), Escherichia coli (MTCC 4296), Klebsiella pneumoniae (MTCC 4030), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 424), Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC 3160), Staphy- lococcus epidermidis (MTCC 435), Vibrio vulnificus (MTCC 1146) and Salmonella typhimurium (MTCC 3216) were procured from the Microbial Type Culture Collection (MTCC), Chandigarh, India. All the cultures were grown on nutrient agar plates and maintained in nutrient agar slants at 4◦C.
All the cultures were grown on nutrient agar plates and maintained in nutrient agar slants at 4◦C. Overnight cultures in nutrient broth were used throughout the experiment.
Overnight cultures in nutrient broth were used throughout the experiment. 2.4.2.
2.4.2. Antibacterial assay The antibacterial activity of CMC stabilized silver nanopar- ticles was evaluated using agar-well diffusion method (Kaviya, Santhanalakshmi, & Viswanathan, 2011).

Antibacterial assay The antibacterial activity of CMC stabilized silver nanopar- ticles was evaluated using agar-well diffusion method (Kaviya, Santhanalakshmi, & Viswanathan, 2011). In brief, 20 ml of each sterile Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) medium was poured into the sterile petri plates and kept at room temperature for solidification.

In brief, 20 ml of each sterile Mueller Hinton Agar (MHA) medium was poured into the sterile petri plates and kept at room temperature for solidification. Then, each pathogenic bacterium was swabbed uniformly on to the individual MHA plates using sterile cotton swabs.

Then, each pathogenic bacterium was swabbed uniformly on to the individual MHA plates using sterile cotton swabs. Wells of 6 mm diameter were made on MHA plates using sterile cork borer and in each well 100 (cid:2)l of nanoparticles suspension was poured.

Wells of 6 mm diameter were made on MHA plates using sterile cork borer and in each well 100 (cid:2)l of nanoparticles suspension was poured. The plates were incubated at 37◦C for 24 h and the antibacterial activity of nanoparticles was observed through zone of inhibition (in mm) on the plates.
The plates were incubated at 37◦C for 24 h and the antibacterial activity of nanoparticles was observed through zone of inhibition (in mm) on the plates. 2.4.3.

2.4.3. Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) The minimum inhibitory concentration was determined based on broth dilution method (Ruparelia, Chatterjee, Duttagupta, & Mukherji, 2008).

Determination of minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) The minimum inhibitory concentration was determined based on broth dilution method (Ruparelia, Chatterjee, Duttagupta, & Mukherji, 2008). 5 ml of each nutrient broth containing various concentrations of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles (10–100 (cid:2)g/ml) were taken in sterile test tubes, and were sonicated for 10 min after adding the nanoparticles to prevent aggregation.

Subsequently, the tubes were inoculated with 0.1 ml of freshly pre- pared bacterial suspension and then incubated overnight at room temperature in an orbital shaker at 200 rpm. After incubation, the MIC of each test strain was measured at 600 nm using a spectropho- tometer (SYSTRONICS, Visible-Spectro 105).

After incubation, the MIC of each test strain was measured at 600 nm using a spectropho- tometer (SYSTRONICS, Visible-Spectro 105). The MBC i.e., the lowest concentration of silver nanoparticles that kills 99.9% of the bacteria was also determined through agar plate method (Wang et al., 2012).

3.1.6. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) The atomic force microscopy result displayed the size, shape, surface morphology and distribution of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles (Fig.
Atomic force microscopy (AFM) The atomic force microscopy result displayed the size, shape, surface morphology and distribution of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles (Fig. 4b).

4b). Surface topology of CMC stabilized sil- ver nanoparticles was spherical in shape and also indicated that the particles were well separated showing no agglomeration in the two dimensional micrograph.

Surface topology of CMC stabilized sil- ver nanoparticles was spherical in shape and also indicated that the particles were well separated showing no agglomeration in the two dimensional micrograph. The 3D image of the stabilized nanoparticles showed sharp peaks with lower to higher sizes of the nanoparticles.

The 3D image of the stabilized nanoparticles showed sharp peaks with lower to higher sizes of the nanoparticles. In accordance with these, the bright spots on the surface topology of AFM micrograph indicated the presence of spherical shape silver nanoparticles (Prasad & Swamy, 2013).

In accordance with these, the bright spots on the surface topology of AFM micrograph indicated the presence of spherical shape silver nanoparticles (Prasad & Swamy, 2013). In the present study the size of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles was ranged from 5 to 15 nm.

In the present study the size of CMC stabilized silver nanoparticles was ranged from 5 to 15 nm. The size and shape of the nanoparti- cles depends on the concentration and type of reducing as well as stabilizing agents used in the synthesis (Sharma et al., 2009).
